item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love how the information in this skip is relayed, and the progression of how the Foundation tries to deal with this anomaly is fascinating to read through. All in all, a really clever and well-made article. +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Your account of how the Foundation goes about trying to study & contain this thing is incredibly compelling. I love the different things tried and the unique setup of the containment procedures. Well done, and thank you for this!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a great read. I love the creativity and how it was writtne in a way, even tho it didnt have a description, I could still peice it all together and understand what it was.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1 just for the clerver writing. Didn't quite get it on my own, but the declassification helped

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So the trick to containing this is by using the stupidest and most inept people in the world? Brilliant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I definitely did take a gamble with the format of this article, and that means the rest of the article must meet a significantly higher standard for it to work. Your point about the recovery is an interesting one, and in retrospect I can see my own thought process around how to describe the anomaly being too concrete and inflexible. In the log, the details are admittedly very easy to gloss over, which could yield the effect you describe.

I'm not planning on rewriting the article, but I think some reflection on the narrative/logic characterization of expungement is something worthwhile for me to consider.

Thanks for the feedback.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Your plan to work with expunging a description by having the Foundation throw personnel at it until they can figure out how to contain it isn't a bad idea, and your concept for how the SCP functions is potentially interesting, but in the end the article as a whole feels flimsy.

It's not flimsy from a logical standpoint (as far as I can tell), but a large chunk of what happens following the description is set on detailing a specific and otherwise par-for-the-course series of events in a short timeframe. As a result, it counter-intuitively makes the SCP less interesting, because you try to define it through grounded means. Recovery logs are somewhat of a staple, and it comes off as routine when all the logs effectively do is tell me how the Foundation contain it. I know they also serve to inform the reader of what's going on with the SCP, but that's not the feeling I get when I finish the article.

That said, you know what you're doing because you know why expunged descriptions work and fail, and that you work with your article to make up for the lack of a description. That it doesn't inherently collapse is props to you as a writer. But with articles like Swann's Proposal and 3005 showcasing the narrative angle of expunging a description, and articles like 3942 and 579 showcasing a more clinical and logic puzzle-esque angle, I'm able to see instances of description expunging working in different scenarios. This doesn't successfully land in either angle, so I have to downvote.
My first 100! Glad people are enjoying it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is extremely clever — I admit I had to read the explanation to get it. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is legitimately a great example of redactions/expunging used to great narrative effect. I love trying to piece together what the actual anomaly is using context clues. The whole thing just feels like a puzzle and I love it.

Great job, Aismallard!

Please check that your sensory deprivation helmet is firmly attached and oxygen is flowing before assisting others. Failure to do so may result in [DATA EXPUNGED]. If an issue occurs, please contact command, but do not provide details.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah for sure, I also appreciate when there are nice layman's explanations for the complex scips, since I don't usually pick up what it is the first time through.

I'm glad you picked that up, part of why the D-class have to be so filtered and brain-damaged is because even untrained humans can comprehend a lot of information passively, like you said: location, sound, smell, etc.

re: "Somewhat spoilery"

+ Spoilers
- Spoilers

Haha that's a great headcanon. My original intention in including the toddler was to show it wasn't some kind of completely lethal hazard like a virus: for whatever reason that toddler never really realized what was happening, and so remained safe from its effects.

As for how it actually works, I'm fine leaving that unstated like the object itself, since it's actually not very important to the story. But perhaps it is related to SCP-4096, which is one of the inspirations for the work.1 The core idea came from looking at the Cliché Con list, and trying to figure a good in-universe reason why a highly skilled Task Force would get totally wiped out.

Footnotes
1. Especially the instance on 2018-09-03.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for doing that, because I don't think I'd fully grasped it at first. It's actually so difficult to understand - a good thing in this particular context! - that the true nature of it wasn't entirely clear.

I figured it as (no spoilers here, btw) something that was universally cognitohazardous regardless of how you perceived it, even if just via ambient sound echoes or haptic vibration/touch through your hands or even soles of your feet. So all of those senses had to be extremely well-filtered and damped whilst triangulating the actual location of the anomaly and building some kind of container around it (*hopefully* it's a static object…), and even whilst in the box there's the potential for leakage.

So now the Foundation know they've got *something* deadly locked up, but they don't know what it is, or even if it'll stay there (presumably it not having escaped *yet* is why it's Euclid rather than Keter), and it's dangerous even for unprepared D-class to remain too close to because just by seeing the box they pick up some information about e.g. its position and maximum possible size…

Somewhat spoilery:
+ show block
– hide block

Headcanon is that it's something that the lone toddler made during a cookie-cooking class in the kitchen, whilst being REALLY bad at making cookies but unexpectedly good at creating anart-type objects, and it's only their general obliviousness* (given their age) which protected them from the effects, at least at first…

  • Or more correctly their general incompetence at everything, including noticing what it is they've made and paying attention to it. It's a rather odd thing that being good at a job related to the object, including knowing anything about it, would cause you to bleed out from the head, but presumably it invokes some kind of fractal restructuring of the part of the brain responsible for memory and procedure…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Resident D-class may request supplies or additional personnel, provided no justification is given.

I had to stop and reread this sentence, because it's just so counterintuitive. Really set the tone for the rest of the entry. Well done! :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've heard of "show don't tell" but this is ridiculous. Like the text-based version of SCP 2521, but with far more examples and testing, which I honestly really prefer.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The particular effects of those two amnestics were necessary for the containment, which is why I linked it.

The idea is that as it is infohazardous, a verified or complete description is not safe. However, you can intuit the anomalous effects from its recovery, also allowing you to see what the Foundation's thought process was as it was trying to determine what the anomaly was.

Edit: I posted a laconic explanation of the anomaly in my author post.

item.title: Author post
item.content:encoded:
For Cliché-Con 2019, in Category 2 - Article features a cliché story element.
Clichés:

  • A highly skilled Mobile Task Force gets steamrolled.
  • D-Class = Disposable Class

Thanks to wctaiwan, Rounderhouse, and Ensophos for critiques.

+ Spoilers
- Spoilers

It's an infohazard that reverses "competent" and "incompetent" whenever you work with information about it. As evident in the recovery, being too knowledgable about it (a form of competence) is fatal.

As such, the D-class who attend to it must be very unable to handle it normally, be unable to improve their skill at containing it, and cannot communicate anything substantial about it to the outside world.

What the object actually is is unstated, and is not knowable in-universe.

There is now also a declassification! (also spoilers)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm having some trouble wrapping my head around this one. I'm second guessing myself for most likely missing something.
